title: Regions Apart: The Four Societies of Canada and the United States By Edward Grabb and James Curtis Oxford University Press, 2010, 344 pages. $27.95
abstract: In Regions Apart, Edward Grabb and James Curtis compile an overwhelming amount of evidence to demonstrate that Canada and the United States are fundamentally similar, and that it is the regional differences that really matter. English Canada, and what the authors call the American North, are very similar, while Quebec and the American South are subsocieties with distinct cultural and institutional histories that continue to matter today. The within nation differences are often greater than the differences between the two countries, and the two subsocieties have over time pulled the American North and English Canada further apart from what was essentially one nation. Grabb and Curtis's goal is to refute the myths of difference between the two countries, and particularly the scholars, like Seymour Lipset, who have built careers on lending these myths an academic validity. In doing so, they demonstrate an enviable breath of perspective conceptually and temporally, drawing on classical theoretical works, historical research, a myriad of contemporary studies and accounts and their own empirical research comparing the two countries.
